title: 28 Years Later The Bone Temple Review: 2026's First Great Horror Movie
text: Director Nia DaCosta and screenwriter Alex Garland's latest installment shifts from zombie-adjacent horror toward folk horror territory. The film follows Spike as he gets drawn into a Satanist cult led by the charismatic Sir Lord Jimmy Crystal. David Ehrlich gives the film a B+ grade. Fiennes delivers the film's most compelling element as Dr. Ian Kelson, a survivalist medic who has constructed a monument to death from human skeletons. He is described as sinewy and feral in appearance but erudite in manner, bringing both scientific rationality and mystical elements to the role. Fiennes performs with committed intensity, even lip-syncing to Iron Maiden's The Number of the Beast. A subplot involving Kelson's relationship with an infected specimen named Samson introduces surprising tenderness to the series. The doctor attempts communication and potential treatment through morphine darts, exploring whether memories persist post-infection. Hildur Gudnadottir's horror score ranges from ecclesiastical passages to unsettling drones. Cinematographer Sean Bobbitt creates dynamic visuals. The reviewer found the Satanist cult storyline more repugnant than scary or compelling. Jack O'Connell's menacing performance feels derivative of his work in Sinners. The narrative solidifies when both storylines converge in the third act with an amusing and consequential encounter.
